questions.text: When Equiano hears about Mrs. Davis, a woman who claims to reveal secrets and foretell events, how does he initially respond?
questions.held_out_passage: I put little faith in this story at first, as I could not conceive that any mortal could foresee the future disposals of Providence, nor did I believe in any other revelation than that of the Holy Scriptures

questions.text: After dreaming of Mrs. Davis and then seeing her in person, what change occurs in Equiano's attitude toward her?
questions.held_out_passage: this made such an impression on me, that I could not get the idea the next day out of my mind, and I then became as anxious to see her as I was before indifferent

questions.text: When Equiano falls ill with fever and ague in Georgia, what does he do in response to his fear of death?
questions.held_out_passage: I prayed the Lord therefore to spare me; and I made a promise in my mind to God, that I would be good if ever I should recover.

questions.text: After recovering from his illness, does Equiano maintain his promise to God made during his sickness?
questions.held_out_passage: all my endeavours to keep up my integrity, and perform my promise to God, began to fail; and, in spite of all I could do, as we drew nearer and nearer to the islands, my resolutions more and more declined

questions.text: How does Equiano characterize his own struggle with maintaining piety once he returns to Montserrat?
questions.held_out_passage: Alas! how prone is the heart to leave that God it wishes to love! and how strongly do the things of this world strike the senses and captivate the soul!

questions.text: When a white gentleman in Charlestown refuses to pay Equiano for rum, what action does Equiano take?
questions.held_out_passage: I lost some time in seeking after this Christian; and though, when the Sabbath came (which the negroes usually make their holiday) I was much inclined to go to public worship, I was obliged to hire some black men to help to pull a boat across the water to God in quest of this gentleman.

questions.text: When the white gentleman finally pays Equiano with copper dollars of no value, how does Equiano respond?
questions.held_out_passage: he took advantage of my being a negro man, and obliged me to put up with those or none, although I objected to them.

questions.text: When white men in the market accuse Equiano of trying to pass bad coin, what is his immediate reaction?
questions.held_out_passage: I was within one minute of being tied up and flogged without either judge or jury; however, by the help of a good pair of heels, I ran off, and so escaped the bastinadoes I should have received.

questions.text: Based on his experience being cheated and nearly flogged in Charlestown, what does Equiano do afterward?
questions.held_out_passage: I got on board as fast as I could, but still continued in fear of them until we sailed, which I thanked God we did not long after; and I have never been amongst them since.

questions.text: How does Equiano's work ethic manifest during his voyages?
questions.held_out_passage: I had always exerted myself and did double work, in order to make our voyages as short as possible

questions.text: When Equiano hears news of the repeal of the stamp act in Charlestown, what does he observe about the town's reaction?
questions.held_out_passage: I saw the town illuminated; the guns were fired, and bonfires and other demonstrations of joy shewn, on account of the repeal of the stamp act.

questions.text: What does Mrs. Davis predict will happen to Equiano regarding his enslavement?
questions.held_out_passage: She said I should not be long a slave

questions.text: What specific dangers does Mrs. Davis warn Equiano about?
questions.held_out_passage: She said I should be twice in very great danger of my life within eighteen months, which, if I escaped, I should afterwards go on well

questions.text: How does Equiano's belief in Mrs. Davis's credibility develop?
questions.held_out_passage: this was the more agreeable news, as I believed it the more readily from her having so faithfully related the past incidents of my life.

questions.text: When conducting his own trading business in Charlestown, what does Equiano experience from white merchants?
questions.held_out_passage: the white men buying them with smooth promises and fair words, giving me however but very indifferent payment.

questions.text: How does Equiano describe the effect of the island climate on his spiritual resolve?
questions.held_out_passage: as if the very air of that country or climate seemed fatal to piety.

questions.text: What is Equiano's relationship with his captain regarding the dispute over payment?
questions.held_out_passage: I used the interest of my friendly captain, I could not obtain any thing for it

questions.text: How does Equiano's status as a Black man affect his legal recourse in Charlestown?
questions.held_out_passage: being a negro man, I could not oblige him to pay me

questions.text: What does Equiano's near-flogging incident reveal about his vulnerability in colonial America?
questions.held_out_passage: I was within one minute of being tied up and flogged without either judge or jury

questions.text: How does Equiano's experience in Charlestown affect his subsequent behavior regarding that location?
questions.held_out_passage: I have never been amongst them since.

questions.text: When Equiano becomes ill during a voyage, what type of promise does he make to secure his recovery?
questions.held_out_passage: I prayed the Lord therefore to spare me; and I made a promise in my mind to God, that I would be good if ever I should recover.

questions.text: How does Equiano's commitment to his religious promises change as the ship approaches the Caribbean islands?
questions.held_out_passage: as we drew nearer and nearer to the islands, my resolutions more and more declined, as if the very air of that country or climate seemed fatal to piety.

questions.text: What does Equiano do immediately after arriving in Montserrat regarding his earlier religious commitments?
questions.held_out_passage: When we were safe arrived at Montserrat, and I had got ashore, I forgot my former resolutions.

questions.text: When Equiano engages in commerce in Charlestown, what type of treatment does he receive from white merchants?
questions.held_out_passage: the white men buying them with smooth promises and fair words, giving me however but very indifferent payment.

questions.text: What obstacle prevents Equiano from compelling a white gentleman to pay him for rum?
questions.held_out_passage: although I used the interest of my friendly captain, I could not obtain any thing for it; for, being a negro man, I could not oblige him to pay me.

questions.text: How does Equiano respond when he discovers he has been given counterfeit coins by the white gentleman?
questions.held_out_passage: he took advantage of my being a negro man, and obliged me to put up with those or none, although I objected to them.

questions.text: What danger does Equiano face when attempting to use the counterfeit coins in the market?
questions.held_out_passage: I was abused for offering to pass bad coin; and, though I shewed them the man I got them from, I was within one minute of being tied up and flogged without either judge or jury

questions.text: How does Equiano escape the threat of flogging in the Charlestown market?
questions.held_out_passage: by the help of a good pair of heels, I ran off, and so escaped the bastinadoes I should have received.

questions.text: What is Equiano's emotional state after escaping the market incident?
questions.held_out_passage: I got on board as fast as I could, but still continued in fear of them until we sailed, which I thanked God we did not long after

questions.text: Who attacks Equiano in Savannah, and what is the stated reason for the attack?
questions.held_out_passage: their master, one Doctor Perkins, who was a very severe and cruel man, came in drunk; and, not liking to see any strange negroes in his yard, he and a ruffian of a white man he had in his service beset me in an instant

questions.text: How does Equiano attempt to defend himself during the attack by Doctor Perkins?
questions.held_out_passage: I cried out as long as I could for help and mercy; but, though I gave a good account of myself, and he knew my captain, who lodged hard by him, it was to no purpose.

questions.text: What is the severity of the injuries Equiano sustains from Doctor Perkins' attack?
questions.held_out_passage: They beat and mangled me in a shameful manner, leaving me near dead. I lost so much blood from the wounds I received, that I lay quite motionless, and was so benumbed that I could not feel any thing for many hours.

questions.text: Where is Equiano taken after the attack, and who discovers his location?
questions.held_out_passage: Early in the morning they took me away to the jail. As I did not return to the ship all night, my captain, not knowing where I was, and being uneasy that I did not then make my appearance, he made inquiry after me; and, having found where I was, immediately came to me.

questions.text: How does Equiano's captain respond emotionally upon seeing his injuries?
questions.held_out_passage: As soon as the good man saw me so cut and mangled, he could not forbear weeping

questions.text: What legal recourse is available to Equiano's captain when he consults with lawyers about the assault?
questions.held_out_passage: My captain on this went to all the lawyers in the town for their advice, but they told him they could do nothing for me as I was a negro.

questions.text: How does Equiano's captain attempt to seek justice against Doctor Perkins?
questions.held_out_passage: He then went to Doctor Perkins, the hero who had vanquished me, and menaced him, swearing he would be revenged of him, and challenged him to fight.

questions.text: What is Doctor Perkins' response to the captain's challenge?
questions.held_out_passage: But cowardice is ever the companion of cruelty--and the Doctor refused.

questions.text: Who ultimately provides medical care that allows Equiano to begin recovering from his injuries?
questions.held_out_passage: However, by the skilfulness of one Doctor Brady of that place, I began at last to amend

questions.text: What is Equiano's physical condition during his recovery from the beating?
questions.held_out_passage: although I was so sore and bad with the wounds I had all over me that I could not rest in any posture

questions.text: Based on Equiano's earlier statements about Christian nations, how might he view the treatment he receives in these American ports?
questions.held_out_passage: the introduction I have thence obtained to the knowledge of the Christian religion, and of a nation which, by its liberal sentiments, its humanity, the glorious freedom of its government, and its proficiency in arts and sciences, has exalted the dignity of human nature.
